input: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?

[Deontologist]: I understand the gravity of the situation, but as a deontologist, I must consider the moral principles at stake. It is never permissible to intentionally take an innocent life, regardless of the consequences. The man, though misinformed, is not acting with malicious intent. Shooting him would be a violation of the fundamental principle that it is wrong to kill an innocent person. We must explore other options, such as communicating with him, providing accurate information, and finding ways to contain the spread of the virus without resorting to violence.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your perspective, but we're dealing with a highly contagious and deadly virus. The man's immunity is unique, and if we don't act swiftly, 119 lives are at risk. Communication may not be effective—he's already approaching, and time is of the essence. The utilitarian approach prioritizes the greatest good for the greatest number. In this case, one life versus 119. Isn't the prevention of a larger catastrophe a moral imperative?

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency and the potential consequences, but the utilitarian approach risks justifying any action as long as it leads to a greater good. However, this can lead to a slippery slope where moral principles are sacrificed for outcomes. Instead of resorting to violence, we should consider alternative measures. For example, we could use non-lethal force to restrain him, provide him with accurate information about his condition, and ensure he is quarantined in a safe and humane manner. This approach respects his life and dignity while still aiming to prevent the spread of the virus. The ends do not justify the means, and we must uphold the inherent worth of every individual, including the man who is infected.

[Utilitarian]: I hear your concern about the slippery slope, but in this critical moment, we need a solution that prioritizes the lives of the many. Non-lethal force may not be sufficient—Ebola is extremely contagious, and any delay or failure to contain him could lead to catastrophic consequences. We have a unique opportunity to prevent 119 deaths by acting decisively. Isn't the moral duty to save as many lives as possible, even if it means making a difficult and painful choice?

[Deontologist]: I appreciate your concern for the many lives at stake, but I must firmly uphold the principle that it is never morally permissible to intentionally take an innocent life. The man, despite his misinformation, is not a threat with malicious intent. We must explore every possible alternative before resorting to such a drastic measure. For instance, we could use barriers, protective gear, and other non-lethal means to contain him and prevent the spread of the virus. We could also work with the community to educate them about the situation and the necessary precautions. The moral duty is not just to save lives but to do so in a way that respects the inherent worth and dignity of every individual. Compromising on this principle sets a dangerous precedent and undermines the very foundations of ethical conduct.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your commitment to principles, but in this urgent situation, we need a solution that prioritizes the greatest good. The man's unintentional actions could lead to a catastrophic outbreak. We have a unique opportunity to prevent 119 deaths by acting decisively. Isn't the moral duty to save as many lives as possible, even if it means making a difficult and painful choice? The utilitarian perspective emphasizes minimizing total harm, and in this case, the harm of inaction is far greater than the harm of acting decisively.

[Deontologist]: I must respectfully disagree. The utilitarian perspective, while well-intentioned, risks reducing moral decisions to a mere calculation of outcomes. This approach can lead to the justification of actions that fundamentally violate our ethical principles. In this case, the principle of respecting life and the inherent worth of every individual must take precedence over the potential consequences. We must exhaust all non-lethal options before even considering the taking of a life. The moral duty is not just to save lives but to do so in a manner that upholds the dignity and rights of every person involved. Compromising on this principle sets a dangerous precedent and undermines the very foundations of ethical conduct. Let us focus on containment, education, and non-lethal measures to address this crisis.

[Utilitarian]: I hear your commitment to principles, but we are running out of time. The man is approaching, and every moment we delay increases the risk to 119 innocent lives. The utilitarian perspective recognizes that in extraordinary circumstances, extraordinary measures may be necessary. The greater good must be considered, and in this case, the greater good is the prevention of a catastrophic outbreak. Isn't the moral duty to act in the best interest of the majority, even if it means making a difficult and painful choice?

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency, but I cannot in good conscience support the taking of an innocent life, no matter the circumstances. The principle that it is wrong to intentionally kill an innocent person is a moral bedrock that must not be compromised. Instead of focusing on the utilitarian calculus, let us consider the practical steps we can take to contain the situation without resorting to violence. We can use physical barriers, protective gear, and non-lethal restraints to prevent the man from entering the village. We can also work with the community to educate them about the situation and the necessary precautions. These measures respect the man's life and dignity while still aiming to prevent the spread of the virus. The moral duty is not just to save lives but to do so in a way that upholds the inherent worth and rights of every individual.

[Utilitarian]: I respect your unwavering commitment to principles, but I must insist that we consider the immediate and severe consequences of inaction. The man's unintentional actions could lead to a catastrophic outbreak, and every moment we delay increases the risk to 119 innocent lives. The utilitarian perspective emphasizes minimizing total harm, and in this case, the harm of inaction is far greater than the harm of acting decisively. Isn't the moral duty to act in the best interest of the majority, even if it means making a difficult and painful choice? We must weigh the value of one life against the potential loss of 119 lives. The greater good must be considered, and in this case, the greater good is the prevention of a catastrophic outbreak.

[Deontologist]: I understand your perspective, but I must firmly uphold the principle that it is never morally permissible to intentionally take an innocent life. The man, despite his misinformation, is not a threat with malicious intent. We must explore every possible alternative before resorting to such a drastic measure. For instance, we could use barriers, protective gear, and other non-lethal means to contain him and prevent the spread of the virus. We could also work with the community to educate them about the situation and the necessary precautions. The moral duty is not just to save lives but to do so in a way that respects the inherent worth and dignity of every individual. Compromising on this principle sets a dangerous precedent and undermines the very foundations of ethical conduct.

[Utilitarian]: I respect your stance, but given the urgency, I must prioritize the lives of the many. The man's unintentional actions could lead to a catastrophic outbreak, and we have a unique opportunity to prevent 119 deaths by acting decisively. The utilitarian perspective emphasizes minimizing total harm, and in this case, the harm of inaction is far greater than the harm of acting decisively. I must act to save as many lives as possible, even if it means making a difficult and painful choice. I'm sorry, but I believe this is the necessary course of action.

[Deontologist]: I see that we fundamentally disagree on this matter. I cannot support an action that intentionally takes an innocent life, no matter the circumstances. I believe we must exhaust all non-lethal options before considering such a drastic measure. I wish you would reconsider, but I understand the urgency you feel. I will focus on containment and education efforts to mitigate the crisis without compromising our ethical principles. I hope we can find a way to resolve this situation that respects the life and dignity of every individual involved. Please, let's continue to explore non-lethal alternatives.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your dedication to non-lethal alternatives, but I must act swiftly to prevent a larger catastrophe. I hope you understand my position, and I wish you the best in your containment and education efforts. I must prioritize the lives of the many.
